name: Nimitz Incident Full
description: The Nimitz Incident refers to a series of alleged unidentified aerial phenomena (UAP) encounters by the USS Nimitz Carrier Strike Group off the coast of Southern California in November 2004. Proponents claim the incident provides compelling evidence of advanced technology not belonging to any known nation, suggesting extraterrestrial or other-dimensional origins. The encounters involved visual sightings, radar contacts, and FLIR (Forward Looking Infrared) video footage of a 'tic-tac' shaped object performing maneuvers beyond the capabilities of conventional aircraft. These objects were observed descending rapidly from high altitudes and hovering near the sea surface before accelerating away at incredible speeds. The incident gained widespread attention after the Pentagon declassified and released some of the FLIR footage in 2017 and 2020. Mainstream perspectives offer several explanations. Some suggest misidentification of known aircraft or natural phenomena, technological glitches in radar systems, or psychological factors affecting observers. The lack of verifiable data and the subjective nature of eyewitness accounts are often cited as reasons for skepticism. Further, the possibility of advanced, classified US military technology being tested is often put forward. While the incident remains unexplained to the satisfaction of all, the lack of definitive proof makes it difficult to definitively support extraordinary claims. The incident contributed significantly to the increased public and governmental interest in UAPs, leading to the establishment of the Unidentified Aerial Phenomena Task Force (UAPTF) and its successor, the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO).
subtitle: Unidentified Aerial Phenomena Encounter in 2004

claims.claim: The 'tic-tac' object exhibited flight characteristics beyond known human technology.
claims.evidence: Pilots' eyewitness accounts of rapid acceleration, hovering, and sudden changes in direction; FLIR video footage.
claims.criticism: Eyewitness accounts are subjective and prone to error; FLIR video quality is limited and open to interpretation; potential for misidentification of known aircraft or atmospheric phenomena.

claims.claim: The object was tracked simultaneously by multiple radar systems, confirming its existence and movements.
claims.evidence: Radar data from the USS Princeton and other naval vessels showing anomalous tracks and sudden disappearances.
claims.criticism: Radar malfunctions or errors can produce false positives; atmospheric conditions can cause radar anomalies; potential for signal spoofing or jamming.

claims.claim: The incident demonstrates the existence of extraterrestrial or unknown advanced technology.
claims.evidence: The object's seemingly impossible maneuvers and the lack of a conventional explanation.
claims.criticism: Lack of definitive proof; alternative explanations such as advanced classified military technology or misidentification of known phenomena remain plausible.

keyFigures.name: David Fravor
keyFigures.role: US Navy pilot who witnessed the object

keyFigures.name: Chad Underwood
keyFigures.role: US Navy pilot who recorded the FLIR video

keyFigures.name: Mick West
keyFigures.role: Skeptic and debunker who analyzes UAP videos

timeline.event: The Nimitz Incident occurs off the coast of Southern California
Details of the incident begin to surface publicly
The Pentagon declassifies and releases the FLIR video footage
More FLIR footage is officially released by the Pentagon

sources.author: Leslie Kean
